definition: To make something unclear, unintelligible, or difficult to understand, often deliberately.
usage_notes: Often implies intentional lack of clarity
Common in formal, analytical, or technical writing
Carries a negative connotation — suggests the speaker is hiding something

etymology.origin_summary: From Latin 'obfuscare' — to darken. 'ob-' (over) + 'fuscare' (to make dark), from 'fuscus' (dark).

examples: The report obfuscated the core issue with layers of technical jargon.
Politicians often obfuscate their positions to avoid commitment.

confusions.difference: 'Complicate' means to make more complex; 'obfuscate' means to make less clear, often on purpose.
'Obscure' can be accidental; 'obfuscate' usually implies deliberate effort.
